original_abstract: Multicomponent reactions (MCRs) represent a cornerstone of modern sustainable chemistry, offering high atom economy and procedural efficiency through the one-pot synthesis of complex molecular architectures. Despite their long history and widespread application in diversity-oriented synthesis, the precise mechanistic pathways of many fundamental MCRs remain subjects of investigation and academic debate. This review provides a comprehensive analysis of the current mechanistic understanding of five quintessential MCRs: the Biginelli, Hantzsch, Mannich, Passerini, and Ugi reactions. We critically evaluate the experimental and theoretical evidence underpinning various proposed reaction pathways, including the roles of iminium, enamine, and Knoevenagel intermediates. By synthesizing findings from NMR spectroscopy, mass spectrometry, and density functional theory (DFT) calculations, this manuscript highlights how reaction conditions, catalysts, and solvent effects dictate mechanistic preferences. Additionally, we address persistent controversies and side-reaction competitions, offering insights into the rational design of more efficient, selective, and environmentally benign catalytic systems for the production of biologically active compounds and industrial intermediates.
humanized_abstract: Multicomponent reactions (MCRs) stand as one of the cornerstones of sustainable chemistry due to their high atom economy and high procedural efficiency toward the synthesis of complex molecules in a single step. Although MCRs have been developed over many decades and are widely used in diversity-oriented synthesis, the detailed mechanisms of many of the basic MCRs remain a topic of discussion in the scientific community. This review aims to summarize the current understanding of mechanisms for five of the most basic MCRs, namely Biginelli, Hantzsch, Mannich, Passerini, and Ugi reactions. Based on experimental and theoretical evidence, the occurrence of different mechanisms for these reactions is reviewed and discussed, taking into account the roles of iminium and enamine species as well as Knoevenagel products. The experimental data, including NMR and MS, as well as the predictions of density functional theory (DFT) calculations are integrated and used to discuss the influence of reaction conditions, catalysts, and solvents on the mechanism of each reaction. Finally, some of the hot points and side reactions are discussed in order to give some possible strategies for the development of new catalysts with higher efficiency, selectivity, and sustainability for the production of valuable chemicals and pharmaceuticals.
